title: A Pour-Over Setup That Punches Above $600 — Grinder First
summary: You said splurge on the grinder, so I put ~half the budget there — grind quality is the single biggest lever in pour-over, full stop. The rest is a clean, no-nonsense brewing kit built around the Hario V60, which is the most forgiving and most-documented dripper on earth. I assumed you already have a decent gooseneck-or-not situation unclear, so I included one; skip it if you have one and reallocate to beans.

sections.name: The grinder (where the money goes)
sections.purpose: A burr grinder good enough that you'll taste the difference in every cup and won't outgrow it.

products.name: Encore ESP
products.why_this: Honest budget pick if you want to keep more cash for beans and accessories — 40 stepped settings, great pour-over range, and Baratza's parts/repair support is legendary. Mention this option if the splurge pick feels like overkill.

products.name: Ode Brewer Grinder Gen 2
products.why_this: Since you said splurge on the grinder, this is the pour-over specialist's pick — 64mm flat burrs, near-zero retention, and stepless-feeling 31 settings dialed for brewed coffee. It's the clear sweet spot in your budget for a pour-over-first setup.

sections.name: The brewer
sections.purpose: The dripper, filters, and server that actually make the coffee.

products.name: V60 Ceramic Dripper Size 02
products.why_this: The reference pour-over dripper — every recipe on YouTube and every café guide is written for this cone. Ceramic holds temperature better than plastic for the splurge-tier vibe.

products.name: V60 Tabbed Paper Filters 02 (100ct, white)
products.why_this: Use the bleached/white ones — they don't impart papery taste like the natural/brown filters do. Tabbed makes them easier to grab.

products.name: V60 Range Server 600ml
products.why_this: Matches the V60 02 perfectly, microwave-safe, and the spout pours cleanly into a mug or two. Boring but correct.

sections.name: Kettle + scale (the controls)
sections.purpose: Precision pouring and timing — pour-over lives or dies here.

products.name: Stagg EKG Electric Pour-Over Kettle
products.why_this: Variable temp to 1°F, gooseneck spout that gives you actual control over flow rate, and a built-in brew stopwatch. If you already own a gooseneck kettle, skip this and put the money toward better beans or the Ode.

products.name: Black Mirror Basic+ Scale
products.why_this: 0.1g resolution with a fast-responding sensor and built-in timer — the affordable scale that the specialty coffee crowd actually uses. Acaia is the splurge alternative but doesn't taste any better.

next_steps: Order a bag of freshly-roasted single origin from a roaster like Onyx, Sey, or a local one — coffee within 2–4 weeks of roast date matters more than gear.
Start with James Hoffmann's 'Ultimate V60 Technique' on YouTube as your default recipe — 15g coffee, 250g water, 4 pours.
If the Stagg EKG pushes you over budget, drop it — a stovetop Hario Buono gooseneck is $60 and works fine while you save.
